title: API-First Architecture for Platforms

seo.title: API-First Architecture for Platforms | Baalvion
seo.description: Contracts, versioning, gateways, governance and developer experience — how to design API-first platforms that scale, through the lens of the Baalvion Operating System.

author: Baalvion Engineering
excerpt: API-first means the contract is the product. Here is how contracts, versioning, gateways, governance and developer experience combine into platforms that scale — and how Baalvion applies them across BOS.

blocks.text: The API is the product, not an afterthought

Most systems grow an API by accident. A team builds a feature, ships a UI, and then exposes a few endpoints because a second consumer needs them. The contract is whatever the code happened to emit that quarter, and every new consumer is a negotiation with the implementation. API-first inverts that order: the interface is designed and agreed before any implementation exists, and the running service is merely one conformant provider of a contract that the organisation treats as a durable asset. The distinction is not cosmetic. When the contract comes first, frontend, mobile, partner integrations and internal services can all develop in parallel against a stable, mocked specification, and the platform stops being shaped by whichever team happened to write the handler.

For a platform builder this is not optional. At Baalvion Industries we run the Baalvion Operating System (BOS) — a multi-tenant infrastructure that unifies commerce, finance, compliance, logistics and intelligence across 198 markets and 180+ jurisdictions, serving 125+ active partners and 500K+ transactions. Those five layers — Infrastructure, Intelligence, Governance, Commerce and Finance — cooperate exclusively through APIs. There is no shared database to reach across, no private back channel between services. The contract between layers is the only coupling, which means the discipline around those contracts is the difference between a platform that composes and a distributed monolith that merely looks like one. This article walks through the five concerns that make API-first real: contracts, versioning, gateways, governance and developer experience — and the trade-offs each one forces.
Contracts: design-first specifications as source of truth

The contract is a machine-readable specification authored before the implementation — OpenAPI for REST and event payloads, Protobuf for gRPC, AsyncAPI for streaming and message-driven interfaces. Authoring it first changes the engineering economics. From a single OpenAPI document you generate server stubs, typed clients in every consumer language, request and response validators, and a mock server that frontend and partner teams can build against on day one. The specification, checked into version control and reviewed like any other artefact, becomes the single source of truth that the implementation must satisfy — not a description scraped out of code after the fact.

The trade-off worth naming honestly is REST versus gRPC. JSON over HTTP with OpenAPI is universally tooled, human-readable, cache-friendly and trivial for a partner to call with curl — the right default for external and partner-facing surfaces. gRPC with Protobuf gives you compact binary framing, native streaming, generated clients and stronger schema-evolution rules — the better choice for high-throughput internal service-to-service traffic where latency and payload size matter. We do not pick one religion. Inside BOS, the settlement and ledger engines speak gRPC to each other for the performance, while the partner-facing trade and compliance APIs are REST so an integrator anywhere in our 180+ jurisdictions can adopt them without a specialised toolchain. The rule that holds across both: design the contract first, validate every request and response against the schema at the boundary, and never let an undocumented field leak into production — for a compliance-first platform handling KYC/AML and AES-256-encrypted data, an undocumented field is an audit liability, not a convenience.

items: Specification before code — author OpenAPI, Protobuf or AsyncAPI first, review it like source, and generate stubs, clients, validators and mocks from it.
Validate at the boundary — reject any request or response that does not conform to the schema; the contract is enforced, not aspirational.
Choose the protocol per surface — REST/OpenAPI for external and partner APIs, gRPC/Protobuf for high-throughput internal traffic, AsyncAPI for events.
Contract tests in CI — provider and consumer tests (Pact-style) fail the build when an implementation drifts from the agreed interface.

blocks.text: Versioning: evolving without breaking consumers

An API with external consumers is a promise you cannot quietly retract. Once a partner has integrated, a breaking change is an outage you scheduled for them. The discipline is to distinguish additive change from breaking change and to make additive change the overwhelming default. Adding an optional field, a new endpoint or a new enum value a tolerant client ignores is safe. Removing a field, renaming it, tightening validation or changing a status code is breaking — and breaking changes require a new explicit version, a deprecation window, and a migration path, never a silent swap.

There are three common versioning strategies and each carries cost. URI versioning (/v1/, /v2/) is the most explicit and the easiest for a partner to reason about, at the price of route duplication. Header or media-type versioning keeps URLs clean but is invisible in a browser and easy to get wrong in caches and gateways. Evolution-only versioning — never breaking, only adding, as Protobuf field numbering and tolerant readers encourage — avoids version proliferation entirely but demands real rigour about backward compatibility. We use URI versioning on the external BOS trade and finance APIs because partners across 180+ jurisdictions need an unambiguous, copy-pasteable contract, and we run old and new versions side by side behind the gateway with usage telemetry so we can see exactly who still calls a deprecated version before we sunset it. Deprecation is communicated in advance — a documented sunset date, a Deprecation and Sunset header on responses, and direct notice to affected integrators — so retirement is a planned event, not a surprise. This is the same backward-compatibility rigour described in [how Baalvion builds scalable software](/news/tech/how-baalvion-builds-scalable-software).
Gateways: the policy and traffic edge

Once you have many services behind many contracts, you need one place to enforce the cross-cutting concerns that should never be reimplemented per service. That is the API gateway. It terminates TLS, authenticates and authorises requests, applies rate limiting and quotas, routes to the right backend version, and emits the telemetry that makes the whole edge observable. Centralising these concerns is what stops every service team from writing their own — subtly different, inevitably weaker — version of authentication and throttling. In a multi-tenant platform the gateway is also where tenant identity is resolved and stamped onto the request, so that downstream services and the data layer can enforce per-tenant isolation with confidence.

The gateway is genuinely cross-cutting, so be precise about what belongs there and what does not. Authentication, coarse authorisation, rate limiting, request shaping, version routing and observability belong at the edge. Business logic does not — a gateway stuffed with domain rules becomes a new monolith and a single point of failure with deployment coupling across every team. We keep the BOS gateway thin and policy-driven: it validates the OAuth2 or mTLS credential, applies the tenant's rate-limit and quota plan, routes /v1 and /v2 traffic to the correct service version, and records structured access telemetry that feeds our Governance layer's audit trail. Fine-grained authorisation — can this tenant settle in this currency in this jurisdiction — lives in the service that owns the decision, close to the data and the policy that governs it. That separation is part of the broader edge discipline we describe in our work on [microservices versus monolith](/news/tech/microservices-vs-monolith) and the resilience patterns in [cloud-native architecture](/news/tech/cloud-native-architecture).

items: Belongs at the gateway — TLS termination, authentication, rate limiting and quotas, version routing, request/response shaping, and access telemetry.
Does not belong at the gateway — domain business logic, fine-grained per-resource authorisation, or anything that couples a team's release to the edge.
Multi-tenant edge — resolve and stamp tenant identity at the gateway so downstream isolation is enforceable and auditable.
Fail safe — circuit breaking, timeouts and graceful degradation at the edge so a sick backend degrades one route rather than collapsing the platform.

blocks.text: Governance: consistency at scale

One well-designed API is a craft problem. Two hundred APIs across dozens of teams is a governance problem. Without shared standards you get a platform where every service invented its own pagination scheme, its own error envelope, its own date format and its own auth header — and every consumer pays the integration tax of learning each one. API governance is the practice of making consistency the path of least resistance rather than a code-review argument. The mechanism is automation, not a review board: a published style guide, a shared error and pagination contract, and a linter — Spectral is the common choice — that runs in CI against every specification and fails the build when a service breaks the house rules.

Governance that lives only in a wiki is governance that does not happen. The patterns that actually scale are a centralised API style guide expressed as machine-enforceable rules, a design-review gate on the specification before implementation starts (cheap to change a YAML file, expensive to change a shipped contract), a single canonical error model so every consumer parses failures the same way, and a registry or catalogue so teams discover and reuse existing APIs instead of duplicating them. For BOS this is inseparable from compliance: consistent, validated, fully documented contracts are what make the platform auditable against SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001 and GDPR. An undocumented or non-conformant endpoint is not just untidy — it is an ungoverned data path, which our Governance layer is specifically designed to prevent. We extend the same governance posture to the partner-facing surfaces we build for clients in regulated sectors through our [finance](/industries/finance) and [enterprise software](/services/enterprise-software) practice.
Developer experience: adoption is the real metric

An API that is technically correct but painful to adopt fails quietly. The measure of an API-first platform is not how elegant the contract reads to its authors but how fast a competent engineer who has never seen it can make a successful authenticated call. That is developer experience, and it is an engineering deliverable, not documentation polish. The components are concrete: reference documentation generated from the live specification so it can never drift; a sandbox environment with seeded test data and obvious test credentials; SDKs generated from the contract in the languages consumers actually use; clear, consistent error messages that say what went wrong and how to fix it; and a changelog that treats consumers as partners rather than surprising them.

There is a real trade-off between expressive power and adoption velocity. The most flexible API — every field optional, every behaviour configurable — is often the hardest to start with, because a newcomer faces a wall of choices before the first successful request. We bias toward sensible defaults and a fast first call: an interactive 'try it' console on the docs, idempotency keys documented prominently for the money-moving endpoints in our [real-time cross-border settlement](/case-studies/real-time-cross-border-settlement) flows, and pagination and rate-limit semantics that are uniform across every BOS surface so learning one API teaches you all of them. The economic argument is simple — for a platform serving 125+ partners, every hour shaved off a partner's first integration compounds across the whole ecosystem. Teams weighing whether to build on a platform contract or stand up their own service should read our comparison of [custom software versus SaaS](/news/insights/custom-software-vs-saas) before deciding where the boundary belongs.
How it fits together at Baalvion

These five concerns are not independent; they form a single discipline. Design-first contracts give you something to version, to route through a gateway, to govern with a linter, and to document for developers — all from one source of truth. Versioning protects the partners who adopted yesterday's contract. The gateway enforces the policy that governance defines. Governance keeps two hundred contracts coherent enough that developer experience is even possible. And good developer experience is the proof that the other four worked. The result for BOS is a platform where Infrastructure, Intelligence, Governance, Commerce and Finance compose through stable, auditable, well-documented contracts rather than entangling through shared internals — the infrastructure-grade, compliance-first posture that lets us operate one unified [platform](/platform) across 198 markets. If you are designing an API-first platform of your own, our [technology consulting](/services/technology-consulting) team starts exactly here: the contract first, the trade-offs explicit, and adoption treated as the metric that matters.

faqs.q: What does 'API-first' actually mean in practice?
faqs.a: It means the API contract is designed and agreed before any implementation is written, and the running service is treated as one conformant provider of that contract. In practice you author an OpenAPI, Protobuf or AsyncAPI specification first, review it like source code, generate stubs, clients, validators and mocks from it, and only then implement. The contract is the durable asset; the code satisfies it.

faqs.q: Should we use REST or gRPC for an API-first platform?
faqs.a: Both, by surface. REST with OpenAPI is the right default for external and partner-facing APIs because it is universally tooled, human-readable and trivial to call. gRPC with Protobuf is better for high-throughput internal service-to-service traffic where latency, payload size and native streaming matter. The constant is that you design the contract first either way and validate against it at the boundary.

faqs.q: How do we version an API without breaking integrators?
faqs.a: Make additive change the default — new optional fields and endpoints are safe; removals, renames and tighter validation are breaking and require a new explicit version. Run old and new versions side by side behind the gateway, watch usage telemetry to see who still calls the old one, and communicate a documented sunset date with Deprecation and Sunset headers before retiring anything.

faqs.q: What belongs in an API gateway and what does not?
faqs.a: The gateway owns cross-cutting edge concerns: TLS termination, authentication, rate limiting and quotas, version routing, request shaping and access telemetry. It should not contain domain business logic or fine-grained per-resource authorisation — those belong in the service that owns the data, close to the policy. A gateway stuffed with business rules becomes a new monolith and a single point of failure.

faqs.q: How do you keep hundreds of APIs consistent across many teams?
faqs.a: Through automated governance rather than a review board. Publish a machine-enforceable style guide, lint every specification in CI (Spectral is common), enforce a single canonical error and pagination contract, gate design review on the specification before implementation, and maintain a catalogue so teams reuse APIs instead of duplicating them. Consistency has to be the path of least resistance.

faqs.q: Why does Baalvion treat developer experience as an engineering metric?
faqs.a: Because adoption is the real measure of an API-first platform. For a system serving 125+ partners, every hour cut from a partner's first successful integration compounds across the ecosystem. We deliver docs generated from the live spec, a seeded sandbox, generated SDKs, uniform pagination and rate-limit semantics, and prominent idempotency keys on money-moving endpoints — so the time to first successful call is short and predictable.
